note_physics_1_4_0.js.title: Choosing Your Pivot
note_physics_1_4_0.js.text: ALWAYS take moments about a point where an unknown force acts — this eliminates that force from the equation entirely, simplifying the solution.

note_physics_1_4_0.js.title: Perpendicular Distance Trap
note_physics_1_4_0.js.text: d must be the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the LINE OF ACTION of the force — NOT the distance along the beam. If force is at angle θ to lever arm: d = L sin θ, or use perpendicular component F sin θ.

note_physics_1_4_1.js.title: Stability & Centre of Gravity
note_physics_1_4_1.js.text: An object is stable if the vertical line through its CG falls WITHIN the base area. Two ways to increase stability: (1) lower the CG, (2) widen the base.

note_physics_1_4_1.js.title: Real-World Application: Double-Decker Buses
note_physics_1_4_1.js.text: CG kept low by heavy engine/chassis at base. London buses must pass a tilt test — remain stable at 28° without tipping. If all passengers move to top deck, CG rises and critical tipping angle decreases significantly.

note_physics_1_5_0.js.title: Work ≠ Force
note_physics_1_5_0.js.text: A large force applied to a wall does ZERO work if the wall does not move (displacement = 0). Work requires BOTH force AND displacement in the direction of the force.

note_physics_1_5_0.js.title: Choosing sin or cos
note_physics_1_5_0.js.text: When force is at angle θ to motion: If θ measured from HORIZONTAL (direction of motion) use cos θ. If θ measured from VERTICAL, use sin θ. Always use the component parallel to displacement.

note_physics_1_5_1.js.title: Kinetic Energy Depends on Speed, Not Velocity
note_physics_1_5_1.js.text: KE = ½mv² depends on SPEED (always positive), NOT velocity. A ball thrown upward and one thrown downward at the same speed have identical KE, even though velocities are opposite.

note_physics_1_5_1.js.title: KE Equation Common Error
note_physics_1_5_1.js.text: When using KE = ½mv², only the SPEED is squared — not mass or ½. If asked for 'loss of KE', calculate both values and subtract: ΔKE = KE_initial − KE_final (no negative sign in answer).

note_physics_1_5_2.js.title: Gravitational PE Reference Level Trap
note_physics_1_5_2.js.text: GPE = mgh depends on the chosen reference level (where h = 0). The absolute value of GPE is meaningless — only CHANGES in GPE (Δmgh) matter in calculations. Always define your reference level clearly.

note_physics_1_5_2.js.title: Falling Objects: Energy Conservation
note_physics_1_5_2.js.text: For falling objects, use energy conservation instead of SUVAT: GPE lost = KE gained, so mgh = ½mv² → v = √(2gh). This allows you to find speed without kinematic equations.

note_physics_1_5_3.js.title: Accounting for Energy Losses
note_physics_1_5_3.js.text: In conservation of energy problems involving real materials, some energy is always dissipated as heat and sound. Always mention this when comparing ideal calculated values to experimental results.

note_physics_1_5_3.js.title: Friction & Energy Loss
note_physics_1_5_3.js.text: If questions state 'friction does work', include the friction term: mgh = ½mv² + W_friction, where W_friction = friction force × distance. Do NOT ignore this — examiners specifically test whether you account for it.

note_physics_1_5_4.js.title: Power at Constant Velocity: P = Fv
note_physics_1_5_4.js.text: P = Fv is an alternative form useful when a constant force F moves at velocity v. For a car at constant speed against drag: P = F_drag × v. At higher speeds, drag increases dramatically → power rises steeply (P ∝ v³ for aerodynamic drag).

note_physics_1_5_4.js.title: Unit Conversions for Power
note_physics_1_5_4.js.text: Power is often given in kW or MW in real-world contexts — ALWAYS convert to watts (W) before calculating with other SI units. Also: energy in kWh = power (kW) × time (h), used on electricity bills.

note_physics_1_5_5.js.title: The 100% Efficiency Myth
note_physics_1_5_5.js.text: No real device is ever 100% efficient in practice. Some energy is ALWAYS lost to the surroundings as heat or sound. This statement appears in every exam — use it to show understanding.

note_physics_1_5_5.js.title: Petrol vs Electric Motor Efficiency
note_physics_1_5_5.js.text: Petrol engine: ~25% efficient overall; 75% lost as heat. Electric motor: ~90% efficient. This enormous difference explains the environmental and economic case for electrification — less fuel wasted, lower CO₂ emissions.

note_physics_1_9_0.js.title: Unit Conversion Trap: Density
note_physics_1_9_0.js.text: Always convert cm³ to m³ when using SI units: 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³ (not 10⁻³). When converting volumes, cube the conversion factor: (0.01 m)³ = 10⁻⁶ m³.

note_physics_1_9_0.js.title: Density Determines Floating
note_physics_1_9_0.js.text: Objects float if density < fluid density, sink if > , neutral if = . Common mistake: confusing which density (object vs fluid) determines floating — always use: ρ_object / ρ_fluid = fraction submerged.

note_physics_1_9_1.js.title: Upthrust: Use FLUID Density, Not Object Density
note_physics_1_9_1.js.text: Upthrust = ρ_FLUID × V_submerged × g. ALWAYS use the density of the fluid, never the object density. This is a common exam error — misusing the wrong density entirely changes the answer.

note_physics_1_9_1.js.title: The Iceberg Question
note_physics_1_9_1.js.text: Iceberg: ρ_ice = 917 kg/m³ in seawater ρ = 1025 kg/m³. Fraction submerged = 917/1025 ≈ 89.5%, so only ~10.5% above water. This 90% hidden fraction explains why icebergs are dangerous to ships.

note_physics_1_9_2.js.title: Stokes' Law Domain: Not for High Speeds
note_physics_1_9_2.js.text: Stokes' Law F = 6πηrv applies ONLY to (1) small smooth spheres, (2) LOW speeds, (3) laminar (smooth) flow conditions. For fast-moving objects (cars, skydivers), drag follows QUADRATIC relationship F ∝ v², NOT Stokes.

note_physics_1_9_2.js.title: Exam Clue: Recognizing When to Use Stokes' Law
note_physics_1_9_2.js.text: Tiny ball bearing in thick oil or glycerol → Stokes' Law. Skydiver, falling raindrop, or car on road → use quadratic drag F = ½C_D ρ A v². Examiners test whether you know the LIMITS of each model.

note_physics_1_9_3.js.title: Confirming Terminal Velocity Has Been Reached
note_physics_1_9_3.js.text: Before timing, ensure terminal velocity is reached by comparing times over TWO EQUAL distance intervals. If both times equal, the ball is moving at constant velocity. If second time is shorter, still accelerating — drop ball from higher point.

note_physics_1_9_3.js.title: Temperature Control in Viscosity Experiment
note_physics_1_9_3.js.text: Viscosity of a liquid changes SIGNIFICANTLY with temperature. If temperature rises during experiment, viscosity drops and measured v_t increases. This is a major source of systematic error — conduct rapidly, repeat at constant room temperature.

note_physics_1_10_0.js.title: Hooke's Law Limit: Elastic Region Only
note_physics_1_10_0.js.text: F = kx applies ONLY up to the elastic limit. Beyond that, extension increases without proportional force increase. Always check whether the question is in the elastic region — if not, Hooke's Law fails.

note_physics_1_10_0.js.title: Springs in Series vs Parallel
note_physics_1_10_0.js.text: Series springs: 1/k_eff = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ → k_eff is SMALLER, springs softer (like two weak springs together). Parallel springs: k_eff = k₁ + k₂ → k_eff is LARGER, springs stiffer (support shared). Common error: confusing these — remember series = softer, parallel = stiffer.

note_physics_1_10_1.js.title: Young Modulus: Material Property, Spring Constant: Not
note_physics_1_10_1.js.text: Young Modulus E is a MATERIAL PROPERTY — same for all samples of steel, regardless of size. Spring constant k depends on material AND dimensions (length and cross-section). This is why E is used to compare material stiffness universally.

note_physics_1_10_1.js.title: Why Materials Have Such Different E Values
note_physics_1_10_1.js.text: Steel E ≈ 200 GPa (used in bridges, buildings); Rubber E ≈ 0.01 GPa (20,000× less stiff than steel). These enormous differences explain why steel is used for load-bearing structures and rubber for cushioning — the material selection is determined by Young Modulus.

note_physics_1_10_2.js.title: Biggest Uncertainty: Diameter Measurement
note_physics_1_10_2.js.text: In the Young modulus wire experiment, diameter measurement is the LARGEST source of error because d appears SQUARED in the area calculation: A = πd²/4. A 2% error in diameter becomes a 4% error in area — use micrometer carefully and take multiple readings.

note_physics_1_10_2.js.title: Why Experimental E May Differ from Accepted Value
note_physics_1_10_2.js.text: If your calculated Young Modulus differs from 200 GPa (steel), the diameter measurement uncertainty usually dominates. Take at least 5 micrometer readings at different positions/planes and use the mean. Wire uniformity directly affects accuracy.

note_physics_1_10_3.js.title: Material Behaviour Classification
note_physics_1_10_3.js.text: BRITTLE materials (glass, ceramics) break suddenly with little plastic deformation — short stress-strain curve. DUCTILE materials (copper, steel) have large plastic region — long tails before breaking. Knowing which you're testing tells you what to expect.

note_physics_1_10_3.js.title: Toughness vs Strength
note_physics_1_10_3.js.text: High breaking stress ≠ high toughness. A material can be strong but brittle (breaks at high stress but suddenly). Tough materials have LARGE area under the entire stress-strain curve — absorb lots of energy before fracture. This matters for safety helmets (high toughness required).

note_physics_1_10_4.js.title: Diameter Calculation Error
note_physics_1_10_4.js.text: COMMON EXAM ERROR: using diameter directly in area calculation. Remember A = π(d/2)² = πd²/4, NOT πd². If you forget to halve the diameter before squaring, your answer is off by a factor of 4.

note_physics_1_10_4.js.title: Confirming You Stayed in Elastic Region
note_physics_1_10_4.js.text: Before analyzing data, check that loading and unloading curves overlap on your graph. If they diverge, the elastic limit was exceeded and plastic deformation occurred — data is unreliable. Re-run experiment with smaller loads.

note_physics_1_10_5.js.title: Elastic Strain Energy from Any F-x Graph
note_physics_1_10_5.js.text: Elastic strain energy E_el = ½kx² = ½Fx = area under the force-extension graph. This area formula works for ANY shape (not just linear Hooke's Law). For curved graphs above elastic limit, you can still find elastic PE by calculating the triangular area up to the elastic limit.

note_physics_1_10_5.js.title: Interpreting the Hysteresis Loop
note_physics_1_10_5.js.text: For inelastic materials, loading and unloading curves form a closed loop. The area enclosed by this loop = energy dissipated as heat per loading-unloading cycle. This shows why rubber gets warm when compressed and released repeatedly — energy is lost to heat, not recovered.
